title: Dog Fever, Back Pain, and Frequent Urination: Triage and Causes
meta_desc: Learn why your dog has a fever, back pain, and frequent urination. Discover the urgency level and how to help your vet diagnose the issue quickly.
content_html:
What Is It?

A combination of fever, lower back pain, and increased urinary frequency often indicates an inflammatory or infectious process. Common culprits include a Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) that may have ascended to the kidneys (pyelonephritis), or in male dogs, an infection of the prostate gland. Sometimes, spinal issues like Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) can cause referred pain and changes in bladder control, though a fever more strongly points toward an infection.

Is it an emergency? (Urgency Level: Medium)

This situation is classified as Medium Urgency. It is not typically a 'red-alert' emergency unless your dog is unable to produce any urine at all or is persistently vomiting. However, because a fever indicates an active systemic response, you should seek veterinary care within 12 to 24 hours. Prompt treatment with antibiotics or anti-inflammatories can prevent a standard infection from turning into a life-threatening blood infection (sepsis) or causing permanent kidney damage.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Visual aids are incredibly helpful for a remote triage nurse or your attending veterinarian. A video of your dog moving or attempting to urinate can show the vet if the 'back pain' is actually a hunched posture from abdominal distress. Additionally, a clear photo of your dog’s urine—especially if it is discolored, cloudy, or contains blood—can provide immediate evidence of the severity of the urinary tract involvement.

faqs.question: Can a kidney infection cause back pain in dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the kidneys are located near the lower back, inflammation or infection in these organs often manifests as sensitivity or pain in the lumbar region.

faqs.question: What is considered a fever in a dog?
faqs.answer: A dog is considered to have a fever if their rectal temperature is above 103°F (39.4°C). Normal range is typically 101°F to 102.5°F.

faqs.question: What should I do if my dog stops urinating entirely?
faqs.answer: If your dog is straining but not producing any urine, this is a medical emergency known as a urinary obstruction and you should go to an ER vet immediately.
